content.assessment: Finland's three-tier non-categorical model is the direct comparator invoked by this proposal, and the evidence is genuinely double-edged. On tracking age and comprehensive provision, Finland's reform decoupled support access from rigid categorical diagnosis, matching the proposal's goal. But Finland's inclusion-tier data reveal a substantial gap between formal tier assignment and actual mainstream placement (only 23% of top-tier pupils fully included), and teachers report documentation burden and cost-driven tier assignment in some municipalities — cautionary parallels for Hungary's gatekeeping redesign. Critically, persistent 4%-13% regional variation after Finland's reform is direct evidence that restructuring gatekeeping architecture does not, by itself, resolve county-level disparities, which the proposal's own risk register anticipates.
opportunities: Finland's transition offers a template for sequencing tiered reform with parallel monitoring, provided Hungary addresses documentation burden and municipal incentive distortions proactively
strengths: Direct precedent exists for non-categorical tiered restructuring changing measured identification patterns, supporting transferability in principle
Finland's tier-based system context provides real operational detail (documentation burden, mixed-class time constraints) that can inform anticipated implementation risk in a comparable reform
threats: Persistent large regional variation (4%-13%) after Finland's reform is strong evidence that Hungary's county-level disparities may survive gatekeeping restructuring largely intact, undercutting a key proposal goal

Context-fit limitation: Finland's institutional capacity, municipal autonomy, and demographic profile differ substantially from Hungary's, limiting direct transferability of the tiered model's success conditions
weaknesses: Only 23% full inclusion of top-tier Finnish pupils despite tiered rhetoric shows a persistent gap between formal support architecture and actual mainstream integration, a caution against assuming structural redesign alone changes lived placement

Documented cost-saving rather than pedagogical motives behind tier assignment in some Finnish municipalities directly parallels the risk of fiscally-driven gatekeeping distortion that categorical redesign alone cannot fix
